text.title: Tech Debt Prioritization and Sprint Allocation
text.summary: Priority scoring formula, pay-now/soon/later tiers, and sprint allocation heuristics for strategic debt management.

section.title: Priority Score Formula

rule.statement: Score debt items using Priority = (Impact x Risk x Interest) / Fix Cost, where each factor is 1-5 and Fix Cost is in hours. Sort descending; tackle highest first.
rule.rationale: Multiplicative scoring ensures that a debt item that is high on all three dimensions ranks far above items with mixed profiles. Division by fix cost prevents low-effort cleanups being buried under expensive-but-critical items.

section.p: Impact: how much does this debt slow development (1=none, 5=blocks features).
Risk: probability and magnitude of failure caused by this debt (1=cosmetic, 5=data integrity/security).
Interest: ongoing cost per sprint (complexity spreading, recurring bugs) (1=isolated, 5=compounds daily).
Fix Cost: estimated hours to resolve.

rule.statement: Use git-history hotspots to prioritize: files churned often AND complex are the highest-leverage payoff targets. Run: git log --since="12 months ago" + lizard complexity cross-reference.
rule.rationale: Hotspot analysis from "Your Code as a Crime Scene" (Adam Tornhill) shows that 20% of files cause 80% of bugs. Complexity alone misses stable legacy; churn alone misses complex-but-frozen debt.

section.title: Pay-Now / Pay-Soon / Pay-Later Tiers
section.p: Pay Now (High Priority): security vulnerabilities, data integrity risks, items blocking other development, high ongoing maintenance cost.
Pay Soon (Medium Priority): items slowing feature development, causing frequent bugs, spreading complexity to adjacent modules.
Pay Later (Low Priority): cosmetic issues, low-traffic areas, stable legacy code that is not changing.

section.title: Sprint Allocation

rule.statement: Allocate 15% of each sprint to continuous debt payoff plus a quarterly debt sprint for large systemic items (hybrid approach). Pure 20% continuous allocation works at scale but breaks for 1-2 person teams who need lumpy debt sprints.
rule.rationale: Hybrid outperforms pure continuous for most teams: continuous allocation handles small-incidental debt while the quarterly sprint addresses large architectural items that require more than a half-day of focus.

section.p: Strategy variants: Continuous (20% every sprint) — prevents buildup. Periodic (every 4th sprint) — risk: debt accumulates. Triggered (when threshold exceeded) — reactive, can reach crisis mode. Hybrid (15% + quarterly) — best of both.

rule.statement: Track debt as labels on existing feature tickets, never in a separate debt backlog. Every TODO/FIXME must reference a ticket: TODO(PROJ-123): description.
rule.rationale: A separate debt backlog is where debt goes to die — it decouples debt from feature work, guaranteeing it is never prioritized. Ticket-linked inline comments keep debt visible in the context where it matters.
